metric_value: TPF is adept at handling reservations, but adding new applications is problematic.
notes: Kastner as Aberdeen Group analyst, Boston consulting firm

metric_value: 4 terabytes of information; 400 airlines, 50 car rental companies, 35,000 hotels; peak 5,200 messages/second; 350 million reservations processed in 1996 at average <2 second transaction time.
Next-generation Sabre involves surrounding the core transaction-processing engine with high-performance, special-purpose systems.
Non-airline revenue growth of 15% to 25% a year.
For a long time, we have wanted to migrate out of the TPF environment in order to reduce the time to market and cost of building products. We have not figured out a way to do that yet.
Travelocity runs on a Unix-based Silicon Graphics server and an Oracle database; 1 million users signed up.
June 1997: Sabre and IBM announced a 10-year agreement to develop systems for Hong Kong airlines.

Sabre continues to run core reservations on TPF (now z/TPF) into the 2020s, surrounded by distributed Java/cloud services — exactly the surround strategy described in 1997. Core TPF never fully retired.
Sabre sold Travelocity to Expedia in January 2015 for $280M; Travelocity rebranded as a meta-search frontend for Expedia inventory.
Sabre Corporation IPO April 2014 (NASDAQ: SABR) after private-equity ownership 2007-2014.
